title: Battery & Power Management
description: Doze mode, App Standby Buckets, background execution limits, foreground service types, and battery-efficient patterns

references.title: Android Developers — Optimize for Doze and App Standby
Android Developers — Reduce Network Battery Drain

lesson:
Doze Mode (API 23+)

When the device is stationary, unplugged, and screen-off for a period of time, the system enters Doze mode to save battery. There are two stages:

Screen off + stationary + unplugged │ ▼ (minutes) ┌───────────────────┐ │ LIGHT DOZE │ API 24+ │ ───────────── │ │ • Network blocked│ │ • Jobs deferred │ │ • Alarms deferred│ │ • BUT: still gets│ │ maintenance │ │ windows often │ └────────┬──────────┘ │ (longer inactivity) ▼ ┌───────────────────┐ │ DEEP DOZE │ API 23+ │ ───────────── │ │ • Network blocked│ │ • Jobs/syncs off │ │ • Alarms deferred│ │ • Wakelocks ignored│ │ • Wi-Fi scans off│ │ • GPS off │ │ • Maintenance │ │ windows become │ │ increasingly │ │ RARE │ └───────────────────┘

Light Doze vs Deep Doze: Timing in Detail

Light Doze (API 24+) kicks in within a few minutes of screen-off. The device does not need to be stationary. Maintenance windows are relatively frequent, roughly every few minutes at first. Deep Doze (API 23+) requires the device to also be stationary for a longer period. Once deep doze engages, maintenance windows expand exponentially:

Deep Doze Maintenance Window Timing: DOZE │ maint │ DOZE │ maint │ DOZE │ maint │ DOZE │window │ │window │ │window │ ─────┼───────┼──────────┼───────┼────────────────┼───────┼────────────── 30s gap 1min gap 2min gap 5min ... continues expanding: 5min → 15min → 30min → 1hr → ... (capped at several hours) Each maintenance window: ~30 seconds for ALL deferred work. If your job doesn't finish in time, it's re-deferred.

During each maintenance window, all deferred work fires simultaneously: pending alarms, JobScheduler jobs, WorkManager tasks, syncs, and network access. This batching is intentional — doing everything at once means the radio powers up once rather than repeatedly.

What Runs in Maintenance Windows vs What's Blocked Entirely

Runs in Maintenance WindowsBlocked Even in Maintenance
Deferred AlarmManager alarmsContinuous background location
Pending JobScheduler / WorkManager jobsWi-Fi scans (deep doze only)
SyncAdapter syncsWakelocks (deep doze — they are ignored entirely)
Network access (temporarily restored)Unrestricted sensor access
Pending FCM normal-priority messagesImplicit broadcast delivery (most)

What Still Works in Doze

FeatureLight DozeDeep Doze
High-priority FCMDeliveredDelivered (temporarily wakes device)
AlarmManager setExactAndAllowWhileIdle()Fires (throttled to 1/9 min)Fires (throttled to 1/9 min)
Regular AlarmManagerDeferred to maintenance windowDeferred to maintenance window
Network accessBlockedBlocked
JobScheduler / WorkManagerDeferredDeferred
WakelocksHeldIgnored
Foreground servicesRun normallyRun normally (network still blocked)

Doze Alarm APIs: The Subtle Differences

Two alarm APIs can fire during doze, but they behave differently:

// setExactAndAllowWhileIdle — fires at exact time, even in doze // BUT: rate-limited to approximately 1 alarm per 9 minutes in doze! alarmManager.setExactAndAllowWhileIdle( AlarmManager.ELAPSED_REALTIME_WAKEUP, SystemClock.elapsedRealtime() + 60_000L, // 1 minute pendingIntent ) // setAndAllowWhileIdle — inexact version, also fires in doze // Same 9-minute rate limit applies, but the system may batch it // with other alarms for efficiency alarmManager.setAndAllowWhileIdle( AlarmManager.ELAPSED_REALTIME_WAKEUP, SystemClock.elapsedRealtime() + 60_000L, pendingIntent ) // setAlarmClock — always fires exactly, shown on status bar // NOT rate-limited, but user-visible — only for actual alarm clocks alarmManager.setAlarmClock( AlarmManager.AlarmClockInfo(triggerTimeMillis, showIntent), pendingIntent )
The 9-minute throttle is per-app. If you fire a setExactAndAllowWhileIdle alarm, the system will suppress additional alarms from the same app for ~9 minutes. This means you cannot use it for frequent polling in doze. If you schedule one every minute, only ~1 in 9 will actually fire.

FCM High-Priority: The Only Reliable Doze Wake-Up

Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) high-priority messages are the only reliable mechanism to reach a user's device during deep doze. The reason: the GCM/FCM system process is allowlisted by the Android OS itself. When a high-priority FCM arrives, the system:

  1. Temporarily lifts doze restrictions for your app for approximately 10 seconds
  2. Delivers the message to your FirebaseMessagingService.onMessageReceived()
  3. Grants a partial wakelock for that 10-second window
  4. Allows network access during that brief window
  5. After 10 seconds, doze restrictions snap back into place
Design implication: Use high-priority FCM for time-sensitive notifications (messages, calls). For the actual data sync triggered by the notification, enqueue a WorkManager task — it will run during the next maintenance window or when the user picks up the phone. Do NOT try to do heavy work in the 10-second FCM window.

Testing Doze with ADB

// Check current doze state $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle // Force device into deep doze immediately (skip the waiting) $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle force-idle // Force into light doze $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle force-idle light // Step through doze states one at a time (great for debugging) $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle step // Repeated calls advance: ACTIVE → IDLE_PENDING → SENSING → // LOCATING → IDLE_MAINTENANCE → IDLE (deep doze) // Step through light doze states $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle step light // Exit doze completely $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle unforce // Whitelist your app from doze (for testing — NOT for production) $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle whitelist +com.example.myapp // Remove from whitelist $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle whitelist -com.example.myapp
Testing tip: Use adb shell dumpsys deviceidle force-idle to enter deep doze, and adb shell dumpsys deviceidle unforce to exit. Use adb shell dumpsys deviceidle force-idle light for light doze. The step command is invaluable for watching your app's behavior as doze progresses through each state.

App Standby Buckets (API 28+)

The system assigns each app to a bucket based on how recently and frequently the user interacted with it. The bucket determines how aggressively jobs, alarms, and network are restricted.

BucketCriteriaRestrictions
ActiveCurrently in use, or very recently used. Visible activity, foreground service, or notification tapped.None — full access to jobs, alarms, and network.
Working SetUsed regularly but not currently active. Runs most days.Jobs run with minor delays. Alarms may shift by a few minutes.
FrequentUsed often but not daily. Opened at least once in the last week or two.Jobs limited to ~10 min sessions. Alarms may be deferred up to several hours. High-priority FCM capped at ~10/day.
RareSeldom used — hasn't been opened in weeks.Jobs limited to ~5 min sessions. Alarms deferred up to 24 hours. High-priority FCM capped at ~5/day. Network access restricted.
Restricted (API 31+)App consumes excessive resources, or user has not opened it in months.Can run one job per day. No expedited jobs. Alarms max 1/day. No FCM priority. Essentially frozen.
Query your app's bucket at runtime:
val bucket = UsageStatsManager.getAppStandbyBucket()
But you cannot set your bucket — only the system decides.

Background Execution Limits (API 26+)

Android 8.0 introduced three major restrictions for apps not in the foreground:

1. Service Restrictions

Background services are stopped within a few minutes after the app leaves the foreground. Calling startService() from the background throws IllegalStateException.

// CRASHES on API 26+ if app is in background: context.startService(Intent(context, SyncService::class.java)) // Solutions: // 1. Use WorkManager (preferred for deferrable work) // 2. Use startForegroundService() + call startForeground() within 10 seconds context.startForegroundService(Intent(context, SyncService::class.java))

2. Implicit Broadcast Restrictions

Apps targeting API 26+ cannot register implicit broadcast receivers in the manifest (with exceptions). You must register them at runtime.

// WON'T WORK in manifest for API 26+ targets: <receiver android:name=".ConnectivityReceiver"> <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.net.conn.CONNECTIVITY_CHANGE" /> </intent-filter> </receiver> // Exceptions still allowed in manifest: // ACTION_BOOT_COMPLETED, ACTION_LOCALE_CHANGED, // ACTION_MY_PACKAGE_REPLACED, etc. // For connectivity: use ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback val cm = getSystemService(ConnectivityManager::class.java) cm.registerDefaultNetworkCallback(object : ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback() { override fun onAvailable(network: Network) { /* online */ } override fun onLost(network: Network) { /* offline */ } })

3. Background Location (API 29+)

Apps must request ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION separately from foreground location. On API 30+, the system directs users to Settings to grant it — no in-app dialog. Google Play requires a declaration explaining why.

Foreground Service Types (API 34+)

Starting with Android 14, every foreground service must declare a type in the manifest. Without it, startForeground() throws MissingForegroundServiceTypeException.

// AndroidManifest.xml — REQUIRED on API 34+ <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE" /> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_MEDIA_PLAYBACK" /> <service android:name=".MusicService" android:foregroundServiceType="mediaPlayback" android:exported="false" /> // When starting, specify the type: ServiceCompat.startForeground( service, NOTIFICATION_ID, notification, ServiceInfo.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_MEDIA_PLAYBACK )

Available types: camera, connectedDevice, dataSync, health, location, mediaPlayback, mediaProjection, microphone, phoneCall, remoteMessaging, shortService, specialUse, systemExempted

Each type requires specific permissions. For example, location requires ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION or ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION. camera requires CAMERA permission. Failing to hold the permission at runtime throws SecurityException.
Android 15 restriction: dataSync foreground services are capped at 6 hours. After that, the system calls onTimeout() and you must stop within seconds. Migrate long syncs to WorkManager.

WorkManager & Power

WorkManager is the recommended API for deferrable, guaranteed background work that should survive process death and device restarts. Understanding how it interacts with power management is critical for battery-efficient apps.

Constraints Deep-Dive

WorkManager constraints let you specify the exact conditions under which your work should run. The system will not execute your Worker until all specified constraints are met simultaneously.

ConstraintMethodWhat It Means
NetworkType.CONNECTEDsetRequiredNetworkType()Any network connection (WiFi, cellular, ethernet)
NetworkType.UNMETEREDsetRequiredNetworkType()WiFi or unmetered network — no data charges
NetworkType.NOT_ROAMINGsetRequiredNetworkType()Not on a roaming cellular network
NetworkType.METEREDsetRequiredNetworkType()Specifically requires a metered connection (rare use case)
ChargingsetRequiresCharging(true)Device is plugged in (USB or AC)
Battery Not LowsetRequiresBatteryNotLow(true)Battery above the system's "low" threshold (~15-20%)
Device IdlesetRequiresDeviceIdle(true)Device is idle (screen off, not actively used)
Storage Not LowsetRequiresStorageNotLow(true)Available storage above the "low storage" threshold

Battery-Optimal Constraint Patterns

// Pattern 1: Heavy data sync — photos, video uploads // Wait for WiFi + charging + idle = cheapest possible time val heavySyncConstraints = Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) // WiFi only .setRequiresCharging(true) // plugged in .setRequiresDeviceIdle(true) // not in use .build() // Pattern 2: Important but not urgent — email sync // Any network, but don't drain a dying battery val normalSyncConstraints = Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) .build() // Pattern 3: Database cleanup — local only, no network needed val maintenanceConstraints = Constraints.Builder() .setRequiresCharging(true) .setRequiresDeviceIdle(true) .build()

WorkManager and Doze Interaction

WorkManager does not bypass doze. When the device enters doze, all pending WorkManager tasks are deferred until the next maintenance window. During a maintenance window (~30 seconds), the system fires all pending work simultaneously. If your Worker doesn't complete in time, it is interrupted and re-enqueued for the next window.

Key insight: WorkManager internally uses JobScheduler on API 23+ devices. On older devices (API 14-22), it falls back to a combination of AlarmManager + BroadcastReceiver. Either way, it respects the system's power management — it is not a way to circumvent doze.

Expedited Work (API 31+)

Android 12 introduced expedited work as a replacement for short-lived foreground services. Expedited work runs immediately, even when the app is in the background, and is given higher priority by the system — but it has a quota.

// Expedited work: starts immediately, survives process death // Good for: processing a received FCM, sending a message val expeditedRequest = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<UrgentSyncWorker>() .setExpedited(OutOfQuotaPolicy.RUN_AS_NON_EXPEDITED_WORK_REQUEST) .build() WorkManager.getInstance(context).enqueue(expeditedRequest) // Your Worker must override getForegroundInfo() for backward compat: class UrgentSyncWorker(ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun getForegroundInfo(): ForegroundInfo { return ForegroundInfo( NOTIFICATION_ID, createNotification() // required for pre-API 31 fallback ) } override suspend fun doWork(): Result { performUrgentSync() return Result.success() } }
Expedited work has a quota. If the quota is exhausted, the OutOfQuotaPolicy determines behavior: RUN_AS_NON_EXPEDITED_WORK_REQUEST downgrades to a regular request, while DROP_WORK_REQUEST cancels it. On API 30 and below, expedited work falls back to a foreground service using the ForegroundInfo you provide.

How WorkManager Picks Its Backend

WorkManager Internal Delegation: API 23+ │────→ JobScheduler │ (respects doze, battery optimizations) │ API 14-22│────→ AlarmManager + BroadcastReceiver │ (custom implementation for older devices) │ All APIs │────→ SQLite database for persistence (survives process death and reboot)

Network Radio State Machine

Understanding the cellular radio's state machine is fundamental to building battery-efficient apps. The radio is the second-largest battery consumer on most phones (after the screen).

Cellular Radio State Machine: ┌──────────────────────┐ data │ │ ┌──────────→ │ CONNECTED (HIGH) │ ~300mW │ request │ Full transmit power│ │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ │ ~2-5 seconds of inactivity │ ▼ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ CONNECTED (LOW/TAIL)│ ~100mW │ │ Ready for more data │ │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ │ ~12-15 seconds of inactivity │ ▼ ┌──┴──────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ IDLE │ ~3mW │ Radio powered down │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ Transition IDLE → HIGH costs ~2s setup + ~20s tail time

Every network request triggers the full state machine cycle. The radio stays powered for 12-20 seconds of tail time after the last byte is transmitted, waiting for more data. If no more data comes, it drops to idle. If another request arrives 5 seconds later — the whole cycle restarts.

The Cost of Individual vs Batched Requests

INDIVIDUAL REQUESTS (sending 10 analytics events, 1 every 30s): Power ▲ 300 │ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ ██ 100 │ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ ████ 10 │────────────────────────────────────────────────── └────────────────────────────────────────────────── Total radio-on: 10 x ~20s = 200 seconds Total data: 10 x 100 bytes = 1KB BATCHED REQUESTS (sending all 10 events at once): Power ▲ 300 │ ██ 100 │ ████████████████ 10 │──────────────────────────────────────────────── └─────────────────────────────────────────────── Total radio-on: 1 x ~20s = 20 seconds Total data: 1 x 1KB = 1KB Same data. 10x less battery. Batching wins.

Prefetching: Using Active Radio Time Wisely

If the radio is already awake (e.g., you just made an API call), downloading additional data you will need soon costs almost zero additional battery. The radio tail keeps it powered for ~20 seconds anyway — use that time to prefetch.

// BAD: Fetch each page only when user scrolls to it fun loadPage(page: Int) { api.fetchArticles(page) // radio wakes for EACH page } // GOOD: Prefetch the next 2 pages while radio is already active fun loadPage(page: Int) { scope.launch { val current = async { api.fetchArticles(page) } val next1 = async { api.fetchArticles(page + 1) } // free! val next2 = async { api.fetchArticles(page + 2) } // free! displayArticles(current.await()) cache.store(page + 1, next1.await()) cache.store(page + 2, next2.await()) } }
Rule of thumb: If you know the user will need certain data within the next 1-5 minutes, prefetch it now while the radio is active. The marginal battery cost of additional data during an active radio session is near zero — it's the state transitions that kill battery.

WorkManager for Periodic Batched Sync

// Instead of sending each analytics event immediately, // queue them locally and sync every 30 minutes class AnalyticsSyncWorker( ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters ) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { val events = localDb.getPendingEvents() if (events.isEmpty()) return Result.success() return try { api.sendBatch(events) // ONE radio wake localDb.markSent(events) Result.success() } catch (e: IOException) { Result.retry() // automatic backoff } } } // Schedule periodic sync — system batches with other work val periodicSync = PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<AnalyticsSyncWorker>( 30, TimeUnit.MINUTES ).setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ).build() WorkManager.getInstance(context).enqueueUniquePeriodicWork( "analytics_sync", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP, periodicSync )

Network Radio Model & Battery Cost

Every network call on a mobile device has a hidden battery cost: the radio tail.

Single HTTP request battery cost: Power │ (mW) │ 300 │ ██████ ← FULL POWER (~2s) │ ████████████████ ← HIGH TAIL (~5s) 100 │ ████████████████ ← LOW TAIL (~12s) │ ████ 10 │──────────────────────────────────────────────── IDLE └──────────────────────────────────────────────── 0s 2s 7s 19s ~25s Total radio-on time: ~20-30 seconds for ONE request! 10 individual requests = 10 × 25s = ~250s of radio time 10 BATCHED requests = 1 × 27s = ~27s of radio time

The cellular radio has three states: Full Power (transmitting), Tail (staying on in case more data follows), and Idle (low power). Each transition from idle to full costs ~2 seconds of setup and keeps the radio alive for 20-30 seconds even for a tiny packet.

Battery Broadcasts & BatteryManager

Android provides several broadcast intents and APIs for monitoring battery state. Using these to adapt your app's behavior is a key power-efficiency strategy.

Battery Broadcast Intents

ActionTypeWhen Sent
ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGEDSticky broadcastEvery time battery state changes (level, plugged, temperature, etc.). You can read the last value anytime without registering — just call registerReceiver(null, IntentFilter(ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED)).
ACTION_BATTERY_LOWRegular broadcastWhen battery drops below the system's low threshold (~15-20%). Sent once per low-battery episode.
ACTION_BATTERY_OKAYRegular broadcastWhen battery rises above the low threshold after being low.
ACTION_POWER_CONNECTEDRegular broadcastWhen external power is connected (USB, AC, wireless).
ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTEDRegular broadcastWhen external power is disconnected.

Reading Battery State: The Sticky Broadcast

ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is a sticky broadcast — the system retains the last instance. You can read the current battery state at any time without registering a receiver, by passing null as the receiver:

// Read battery state at any time — no receiver registration needed! fun getBatteryState(context: Context): BatteryState { val batteryIntent: Intent? = context.registerReceiver( null, // null receiver = just give me the sticky value IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED) ) val level = batteryIntent?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_LEVEL, -1) ?: -1 val scale = batteryIntent?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_SCALE, 100) ?: 100 val batteryPct = level * 100 / scale val plugged = batteryIntent?.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_PLUGGED, 0) ?: 0 val isCharging = plugged != 0 val chargingSource = when (plugged) { BatteryManager.BATTERY_PLUGGED_AC -> "AC" BatteryManager.BATTERY_PLUGGED_USB -> "USB" BatteryManager.BATTERY_PLUGGED_WIRELESS -> "Wireless" else -> "Not charging" } val temp = batteryIntent?.getIntExtra( BatteryManager.EXTRA_TEMPERATURE, 0 )?.div(10f) ?: 0f // temperature in tenths of a degree C val health = batteryIntent?.getIntExtra( BatteryManager.EXTRA_HEALTH, BatteryManager.BATTERY_HEALTH_UNKNOWN ) return BatteryState(batteryPct, isCharging, chargingSource, temp) } data class BatteryState( val percentage: Int, val isCharging: Boolean, val chargingSource: String, val temperatureCelsius: Float )

BatteryManager API (API 21+)

For programmatic access beyond the sticky broadcast, use BatteryManager directly:

// BatteryManager provides real-time battery properties val bm = context.getSystemService(Context.BATTERY_SERVICE) as BatteryManager // Is the device charging right now? val isCharging = bm.isCharging // API 23+ // Current battery capacity as percentage (0-100) val batteryPct = bm.getIntProperty( BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CAPACITY ) // Instantaneous current draw in microamps (negative = discharging) val currentNow = bm.getIntProperty( BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CURRENT_NOW ) // Average current draw in microamps val currentAvg = bm.getIntProperty( BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CURRENT_AVERAGE ) // Remaining energy in nanowatt-hours val energyRemaining = bm.getLongProperty( BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_ENERGY_COUNTER )

Adapting Behavior Based on Battery State

// Check if the network is metered — avoid heavy downloads on cellular val cm = context.getSystemService(ConnectivityManager::class.java) val isMetered = cm.isActiveNetworkMetered // Combine battery + network checks for adaptive behavior fun shouldPerformHeavySync(context: Context): Boolean { val bm = context.getSystemService(Context.BATTERY_SERVICE) as BatteryManager val cm = context.getSystemService(ConnectivityManager::class.java) val isCharging = bm.isCharging val batteryPct = bm.getIntProperty(BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CAPACITY) val isMetered = cm.isActiveNetworkMetered return when { isCharging && !isMetered -> true // charging + WiFi: go ahead isCharging && batteryPct > 80 -> true // charging + high battery: ok !isCharging && batteryPct > 50 && !isMetered -> true // WiFi, good battery else -> false // defer to WorkManager constraints } }
ConnectivityManager.isActiveNetworkMetered() returns true for cellular networks where data costs money. Use this to avoid large downloads on metered connections — even if the user has a fast LTE signal, they may be paying per MB.

Battery-Efficient Patterns

1. Batch Network Calls

// BAD: Individual requests scattered over time fun trackEvent(event: Event) { api.sendEvent(event) // wakes radio for 25s each time! } // GOOD: Batch and send together class EventBatcher { private val queue = mutableListOf<Event>() fun trackEvent(event: Event) { queue.add(event) if (queue.size >= 10) flush() } fun flush() { if (queue.isEmpty()) return val batch = queue.toList() queue.clear() api.sendEventBatch(batch) // ONE radio wake for all events } }

2. WorkManager Constraints

// Let the system pick the most efficient time val syncRequest = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>() .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) // Wi-Fi only .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) // >15% battery .setRequiresCharging(true) // on charger .build() ) .build()

3. Wakelock Best Practices

// ALWAYS use a timeout — prevents battery drain from leaked wakelocks val wakeLock = (getSystemService(POWER_SERVICE) as PowerManager) .newWakeLock(PowerManager.PARTIAL_WAKE_LOCK, "app:sync") wakeLock.acquire(10 * 60 * 1000L) // 10 minute TIMEOUT try { performSync() } finally { if (wakeLock.isHeld) wakeLock.release() } // NEVER do this — infinite wakelock = battery killer // wakeLock.acquire() // no timeout!
Android Vitals flags apps with wakelocks held >1 hour in the background. This directly affects your Play Store ranking and visibility. Always pass a timeout to acquire().

4. Location Coalescing

// Let FusedLocationProvider batch with other apps' requests val locationRequest = LocationRequest.Builder( Priority.PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY, 60_000L // interval: 60 seconds ) .setMaxUpdateDelayMillis(300_000L) // batch up to 5 minutes .setMinUpdateDistanceMeters(50f) // skip if barely moved .build() // setMaxUpdateDelayMillis enables batching: the system can // hold locations and deliver them all at once, saving battery

Monitoring & Debugging Power Issues

Diagnosing battery drain requires specialized tools. Android provides several layers of instrumentation — from high-level dashboards to low-level system dumps.

Battery Historian

Battery Historian is a visualization tool that reads a device bugreport and displays a detailed timeline of battery-related events: wakelocks, jobs, alarms, radio state, screen, GPS, and per-app battery usage.

// Step 1: Reset battery stats on device $ adb shell dumpsys batterystats --reset // Step 2: Unplug and use the device for your test scenario // (must be unplugged — USB charging skews stats) // Step 3: Capture a bugreport $ adb bugreport bugreport.zip // Step 4: Upload to Battery Historian web tool // Run locally via Docker: $ docker run -p 9999:9999 gcr.io/android-battery-historian/stable:3.1 --port 9999 // Then open http://localhost:9999 and upload bugreport.zip

ADB Battery Debugging Commands

// Per-app battery usage breakdown $ adb shell dumpsys batterystats --charged com.example.myapp // Shows: wakelocks, network bytes, CPU time, sensor usage, // alarms, jobs, GPS time — everything that drained battery // Doze state inspection $ adb shell dumpsys deviceidle // Shows: current state (ACTIVE, IDLE, etc.), whitelist, // next alarm times, maintenance window schedule // Check which wakelocks are currently held $ adb shell dumpsys power | grep -i wake // Find stuck wakelocks that are draining battery // Check current app standby bucket $ adb shell am get-standby-bucket com.example.myapp // Force an app into a specific bucket (for testing) $ adb shell am set-standby-bucket com.example.myapp rare

Android Studio Energy Profiler

The Energy Profiler (part of Android Studio's Profiler) shows real-time estimates of CPU, network, and location energy usage. It highlights:

  • Wake locks: shows acquire/release timeline — easy to spot unreleased locks
  • Jobs and alarms: when they fire and how long they run
  • Location requests: which priority level and how frequently
  • Network activity: correlated with radio state transitions
Open it via View > Tool Windows > Profiler > Energy. It works on API 26+ devices and emulators. While it cannot measure actual milliamps (that requires hardware), it estimates relative energy impact based on system resource usage.

Android Vitals (Google Play Console)

Android Vitals automatically monitors your app in the wild and flags:

  • Stuck partial wakelocks: held for >1 hour in the background
  • Excessive wakeups: >10 wakeups per hour when the app is in the background
  • Excessive background WiFi scans: >4 scans per hour in the background
  • Excessive background network usage: unusually high data transfer while backgrounded
Android Vitals violations directly impact your Play Store ranking. Google treats power-related vitals as a quality signal — apps with stuck wakelocks or excessive wakeups are demoted in search results. Fix vitals issues before adding new features.

Common Power Bugs

BugSymptomFix
Forgotten wakelockBattery drains overnight; Vitals flags stuck wakelockAlways use try/finally + timeout on acquire()
Polling instead of pushConstant network activity; radio never sleepsUse FCM for server-push; WorkManager for periodic sync
GPS at HIGH_ACCURACY when coarse is fineRapid battery drain; GPS icon stuck onUse PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY or PRIORITY_LOW_POWER
Keeping screen on (FLAG_KEEP_SCREEN_ON)Screen never dims; major drainOnly use during active user interaction; clear the flag when done
AlarmManager for repeating tasksDevice wakes up frequently; doze can't helpReplace with WorkManager periodic work
Registering sensors without unregisteringAccelerometer/gyro running continuouslyUnregister in onPause()/onStop()

Power-Aware Architecture Patterns

Building truly battery-efficient apps goes beyond individual API calls — it requires architectural decisions that make power-awareness a first-class concern.

1. FCM Push Instead of Polling

POLLING (bad): App ──request──→ Server (every 5 min, even when no new data) App ←──empty──── Server Radio wakes 288 times/day! PUSH (good): Server ──FCM──→ App (only when new data exists) App ──request──→ Server Radio wakes only when needed App ←──data───── Server

Polling every 5 minutes means 288 radio wake-ups per day — even when the server has nothing new. FCM push means zero radio wake-ups from your app until there's actually new data. The FCM connection itself is shared across all apps on the device and maintained by Google Play Services.

2. Location Batching

// Battery-efficient location tracking for a fitness app val locationRequest = LocationRequest.Builder( Priority.PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY, 30_000L // 30 seconds nominal interval ) .setMaxUpdateDelayMillis(120_000L) // batch up to 2 minutes .setMinUpdateDistanceMeters(10f) // skip if barely moved .setWaitForAccurateLocation(false) // don't wait for GPS lock .build() // The system can hold up to 4 location fixes and deliver // them all at once every 2 minutes, instead of waking the // app CPU 4 separate times

3. Prefetch + Cache Strategy

Download data during optimal conditions (WiFi + charging) and serve from cache later:

// Schedule prefetch during optimal conditions val prefetchRequest = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<PrefetchWorker>() .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) // WiFi .setRequiresCharging(true) // plugged in .build() ) .build() class PrefetchWorker(ctx: Context, params: WorkerParameters) : CoroutineWorker(ctx, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { // Download tomorrow's content while on WiFi + charging val articles = api.fetchTomorrowFeed() val images = articles.flatMap { it.imageUrls } articleDao.insertAll(articles) imageCache.prefetch(images) // download images to disk return Result.success() } } // When user opens app on cellular next morning, // everything loads from cache — zero radio usage!

4. Adaptive Behavior: PowerAwareRepository

A repository that adapts its sync strategy based on current battery and network conditions:

class PowerAwareRepository( private val api: ApiService, private val cache: ArticleCache, private val context: Context ) { private val batteryManager = context.getSystemService(Context.BATTERY_SERVICE) as BatteryManager private val connectivityManager = context.getSystemService(ConnectivityManager::class.java) suspend fun getArticles(forceRefresh: Boolean = false): List<Article> { val isCharging = batteryManager.isCharging val batteryPct = batteryManager.getIntProperty( BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CAPACITY ) val isMetered = connectivityManager.isActiveNetworkMetered // Strategy 1: Charging + WiFi → full sync with images if (isCharging && !isMetered) { return fullSyncWithImages() } // Strategy 2: Good battery + WiFi → sync text, skip images if (batteryPct > 50 && !isMetered) { return syncTextOnly() } // Strategy 3: On cellular → serve from cache, background sync if (isMetered) { val cached = cache.getArticles() if (cached.isNotEmpty() && !forceRefresh) return cached return syncMinimal() // headlines only, no images } // Strategy 4: Low battery → cache only, schedule sync for later if (batteryPct < 20) { scheduleBackgroundSync() return cache.getArticles() } return syncTextOnly() } private fun scheduleBackgroundSync() { val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>() .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.UNMETERED) .setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) .build() ).build() WorkManager.getInstance(context) .enqueueUniqueWork("sync", ExistingWorkPolicy.KEEP, request) } private suspend fun fullSyncWithImages(): List<Article> { val articles = api.fetchArticles(includeImages = true) cache.storeArticles(articles) return articles } private suspend fun syncTextOnly(): List<Article> { val articles = api.fetchArticles(includeImages = false) cache.storeArticles(articles) return articles } private suspend fun syncMinimal(): List<Article> { val articles = api.fetchHeadlines() // lightweight cache.storeArticles(articles) return articles } }
The key insight: there is no single "right" sync strategy. The best approach changes based on the device's current state. A power-aware repository checks battery level, charging status, and network type before deciding how aggressively to sync. When conditions are poor, it defers to WorkManager. When conditions are ideal (charging + WiFi), it goes all out.

5. Reducing Sync Frequency Adaptively

// Adaptive sync interval based on battery state fun scheduleSyncWithAdaptiveInterval(context: Context) { val bm = context.getSystemService(Context.BATTERY_SERVICE) as BatteryManager val isCharging = bm.isCharging val batteryPct = bm.getIntProperty(BatteryManager.BATTERY_PROPERTY_CAPACITY) val intervalMinutes = when { isCharging -> 15L // aggressive when plugged in batteryPct > 50 -> 30L // normal batteryPct > 20 -> 60L // conservative else -> 120L // survival mode } val request = PeriodicWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>( intervalMinutes, TimeUnit.MINUTES ).setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ).build() WorkManager.getInstance(context).enqueueUniquePeriodicWork( "adaptive_sync", ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.UPDATE, // replaces existing schedule request ) } // Re-evaluate when power state changes val powerReceiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { scheduleSyncWithAdaptiveInterval(context) } } context.registerReceiver(powerReceiver, IntentFilter().apply { addAction(Intent.ACTION_POWER_CONNECTED) addAction(Intent.ACTION_POWER_DISCONNECTED) addAction(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_LOW) addAction(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_OKAY) })

questions.title: Doze Mode Behavior
questions.description: Your app schedules a WorkManager job to sync data every 30 minutes. The user puts the phone on a table, screen off, unplugged. After 2 hours, how many syncs have likely completed?
options: 4 syncs (every 30 minutes as scheduled)
0 syncs — all deferred until the user picks up the phone or a maintenance window opens
2 syncs — Doze cuts the frequency in half
4 syncs — WorkManager bypasses Doze restrictions
questions.explanation: In Doze mode, all JobScheduler and WorkManager tasks are deferred to the next maintenance window. The system batches everything together. Maintenance windows become increasingly rare in deep Doze. The syncs will run eventually — either during a maintenance window or when the user picks up the device — but not on schedule. Only high-priority FCM messages and setExactAndAllowWhileIdle() alarms can break through Doze.
hints: Think about what Doze defers vs. what it allows through
WorkManager uses JobScheduler under the hood

questions.title: App Standby Buckets
questions.description: A news app that the user opens once a week is in the Frequent bucket. It uses high-priority FCM to push breaking news alerts. What happens?
options: All high-priority FCMs are delivered instantly, regardless of bucket
High-priority FCMs are capped at ~10/day — excess messages are downgraded to normal priority and deferred
The app is completely blocked from receiving FCM
High-priority FCMs are delivered but limited to 3/day
questions.explanation: In the Frequent bucket, high-priority FCM messages are capped at approximately 10 per day. Messages beyond the cap are treated as normal priority and deferred. In the Rare bucket, it drops to ~5/day. In the Restricted bucket, no FCM priority is granted at all. Only the Active and Working Set buckets have no FCM cap. This is why user engagement matters — it directly affects your ability to reach users.
hints: Buckets don't block FCM entirely, but they throttle it
The quota is different for each bucket

questions.title: Network Radio Tail Energy
questions.description: An analytics SDK sends a small HTTP request (100 bytes) every time the user taps a button. The user taps 15 buttons over 10 minutes. How much total radio-on time does this cause, and what is the efficient alternative?
options: ~3 seconds total — small requests use minimal radio
~15 seconds total — 1 second per request
~375 seconds (6+ minutes) of radio-on time — each request wakes the radio for ~25 seconds
~150 seconds — the radio batches small requests automatically
questions.explanation: Each individual network request wakes the cellular radio from idle, which keeps it powered for ~20-30 seconds (the "radio tail"). 15 requests x 25 seconds = ~375 seconds of radio-on time for just 1500 bytes of data. The fix: batch events in memory and send them in a single request (or use WorkManager). Batched, all 15 events go in one request = ~27 seconds of radio time total. This is the single biggest battery optimization for apps that send frequent small requests.
hints: Think about the radio state machine: idle → full power → tail → idle
The data size doesn't matter — it's the number of separate connections that hurts

questions.title: Wakelock Without Timeout
questions.description: This sync service acquires a wakelock to keep the CPU running during a data sync. If the sync hangs or throws an exception, the wakelock is never released and drains the battery. Fix both issues.

checks.message: Pass a timeout to acquire() to prevent infinite wakelocks
Use try/finally to ensure the wakelock is always released

questions.explanation: Two critical fixes: (1) Timeout on acquire() — if the wakelock is never manually released (crash, hang, logic error), it automatically expires after the timeout. Android Vitals flags wakelocks held >1 hour and penalizes your app listing. (2) try/finally — ensures release() runs even if performSync() throws. Always check isHeld before releasing to avoid RuntimeException from double-release.
hints: What happens if performSync() throws an exception before release()?
How can you prevent the wakelock from being held forever if the code hangs?

questions.title: Foreground Service Types (API 34+)
questions.description: You are adding a music playback foreground service to an app targeting API 34. You declare <service android:name=".MusicService" /> in the manifest without specifying foregroundServiceType. What happens when you call startForeground()?
options: It works normally — the type is optional
The service runs but logs a warning in Logcat
startForeground() throws MissingForegroundServiceTypeException at runtime
The app fails to install on API 34 devices
questions.explanation: Starting with API 34 (Android 14), every foreground service must declare a foregroundServiceType in the manifest. Calling startForeground() without one throws MissingForegroundServiceTypeException. You must also hold the corresponding type-specific permission (e.g., FOREGROUND_SERVICE_MEDIA_PLAYBACK) and pass the type constant to ServiceCompat.startForeground().
hints: Android 14 made foreground service types mandatory, not optional
Think about when the error surfaces — at compile time, install time, or runtime?

questions.title: Background Execution Limits
questions.description: Explain the three major restrictions Android 8.0 (API 26) placed on background apps and the recommended migration path for each.
questions.answer: Android 8.0 introduced three restrictions: (1) Background service limits — calling startService() when the app is in the background throws IllegalStateException. Migrate to WorkManager for deferrable work or startForegroundService() for user-visible tasks. (2) Implicit broadcast restrictions — most implicit broadcasts registered in the manifest won't be delivered to background apps. Register them dynamically with registerReceiver() at runtime, or use explicit broadcasts. (3) Background location — further restricted in API 29+, requiring a separate ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION permission. Use foreground services with the location type for continuous location needs.
questions.explanation: These restrictions were Google's first major push to curb battery drain from background apps. Before API 26, any app could start services, listen for system broadcasts, and poll location indefinitely — even if the user hadn't opened the app in weeks. The restrictions forced the ecosystem toward battery-efficient APIs like WorkManager and runtime-registered receivers.
hints: Think about what a malicious or poorly-written app could do before these limits existed
Each restriction has a recommended replacement API

questions.title: Location Batching for Battery
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a battery-efficient location request that batches updates and skips locations when the device barely moves.
questions.code:
val locationRequest = LocationRequest.Builder(
    Priority.PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY,
    60_000L // request every 60 seconds
)
    .___1___(300_000L)  // batch up to 5 minutes
    .___2___(50f)       // skip if moved <50 meters
    .build()

blanks.hint: This method tells the system how long it can hold location updates before delivering them in a batch
This method sets a minimum displacement threshold — no update if the device barely moved

questions.explanation: setMaxUpdateDelayMillis() enables location batching — the system can accumulate multiple location fixes and deliver them all at once, letting the main processor sleep between deliveries. setMinUpdateDistanceMeters() adds a displacement filter so the app doesn't wake up for trivial movement. Together, these can reduce location-related battery drain by 50-80% compared to unbatched, zero-threshold requests.
hints: One controls HOW LONG the system can delay delivery, the other controls HOW FAR the device must move
Both are methods on LocationRequest.Builder

questions.title: Deep Doze vs Light Doze
questions.description: A messaging app uses a regular AlarmManager.setExact() alarm to poll for new messages every 5 minutes. A foreground service plays music. The phone has been sitting on a desk, screen off, for 1 hour. What is the state of each?
options: Both the alarm and music service work normally
The alarm fires every 5 minutes as scheduled; the music service is killed by Doze
The alarm is deferred to the next maintenance window; the music service continues playing but has no network access
Both are completely blocked until the user picks up the phone
questions.explanation: In deep Doze: (1) setExact() alarms are deferred to the next maintenance window — only setExactAndAllowWhileIdle() or setAlarmClock() can fire during Doze (and even then, throttled to ~1 per 9 minutes). (2) Foreground services continue running — Doze does not kill them — but network access is blocked. The music plays fine (local playback), but if it needs to stream, it will fail. This is a common gotcha: foreground service + Doze = service alive but network dead.
hints: Doze treats foreground services and alarms very differently
Think about what 'deferred' means vs. 'killed'

questions.title: AlarmManager Polling Battery Drain
questions.description: Your app syncs data every 5 minutes using AlarmManager.setRepeating(). On a fully charged phone left idle overnight (8 hours), battery drops 15%. Users are complaining. What is the root cause and how do you fix it?
options: The alarm interval is too short — increase it to 15 minutes and the problem is solved
Each alarm wakes the radio for ~20 seconds of tail time; 96 wake-ups per night = ~32 minutes of radio active time. Replace with WorkManager PeriodicWork with CONNECTED + BATTERY_NOT_LOW constraints
AlarmManager alarms are free — the problem must be in the sync code itself doing too much work
The fix is to use setExactAndAllowWhileIdle() instead of setRepeating() so Doze can manage the timing
questions.explanation: Every 5-minute alarm wakes the device, wakes the cellular radio, and keeps it powered for ~20 seconds (radio tail time). Over 8 hours: 96 wake-ups x 20 seconds = 32 minutes of radio active time — plus CPU wake time for each alarm. The fix is to replace AlarmManager with WorkManager using appropriate constraints. WorkManager lets the system batch your sync with other apps' work during optimal times, and it respects Doze. Adding setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) ensures you won't drain a dying battery, and NetworkType.CONNECTED ensures you only sync when network is available. Option D is wrong because setExactAndAllowWhileIdle is rate-limited but still wakes the radio unnecessarily — the real fix is deferrable scheduling.
hints: Calculate the total radio-on time: number of wake-ups times the tail time per wake-up
Think about what API is designed specifically for deferrable work that should respect battery

questions.title: Battery Broadcasts: Sticky vs Regular
questions.description: What is the difference between ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED (sticky broadcast) and ACTION_BATTERY_LOW (regular broadcast)?
options: They contain the same data but ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is sent more frequently

ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is a sticky broadcast that persists — you can read the last value anytime by registering with a null receiver. ACTION_BATTERY_LOW is a regular broadcast sent once when battery drops below the low threshold.
ACTION_BATTERY_LOW replaces ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED on API 26+ devices
ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is only available to system apps; third-party apps must use ACTION_BATTERY_LOW
questions.explanation: ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED is a sticky broadcast — the system retains the last Intent. You can read the current battery state at any time by calling registerReceiver(null, IntentFilter(ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED)). The returned Intent contains detailed extras: level, scale, plugged state, temperature, health, and voltage. It fires on every battery state change. ACTION_BATTERY_LOW is a regular broadcast sent once when the battery drops below the system's low threshold (~15-20%). It does not persist and carries no extras. Its companion ACTION_BATTERY_OKAY fires when the level recovers. The key distinction is that sticky broadcasts can be queried retroactively, while regular broadcasts must be caught in real-time.
hints: Think about what 'sticky' means for a broadcast — what happens if you register after it was sent?
One gives you detailed battery info on demand; the other is a one-time event notification

questions.title: WorkManager PeriodicWork with Charging Constraint
questions.description: You set up a PeriodicWorkRequest with setRequiresCharging(true) and a 1-hour repeat interval. The device stays unplugged all day (12 hours). When does the work run?
options: It runs every hour regardless — charging is a soft preference, not a hard requirement
It runs once when the constraint has been unmet for too long — WorkManager has a fallback timer
It never runs while unplugged. The moment the device is plugged in, the work runs immediately (since the interval has long passed)
It skips the first 3 intervals, then runs anyway to prevent data staleness
questions.explanation: WorkManager constraints are hard requirements, not suggestions. If setRequiresCharging(true) is set, the work will never execute while the device is unplugged — no matter how many intervals pass. There is no fallback timer or staleness override. The moment the device is plugged in and the constraint is satisfied, WorkManager checks if the interval has elapsed (which it has, many times over) and executes the work immediately. This is by design: constraints ensure your heavy work only runs under optimal conditions. If your work absolutely must run on a schedule regardless of charging state, do not use the charging constraint — use setRequiresBatteryNotLow(true) instead for a lighter battery guard.
hints: WorkManager constraints are hard gates — the work literally cannot run until ALL constraints are met
What happens to a periodic task whose interval has passed when the constraint is finally satisfied?

questions.title: FCM High-Priority During Deep Doze
questions.description: The device is in deep doze. Network access is blocked. Jobs are deferred. Wakelocks are ignored. Yet a high-priority FCM message from your server still reaches the app. How is this possible?
options: FCM uses a special kernel-level socket that bypasses Android's power management entirely
FCM messages are queued on Google's servers and delivered when doze lifts — they don't actually arrive during doze

The GCM/FCM system service is allowlisted by the OS. When a high-priority FCM arrives, the system temporarily lifts doze restrictions for the target app for approximately 10 seconds, granting a wakelock and network access
FCM uses Bluetooth Low Energy to wake the device, which is not affected by doze
questions.explanation: The GCM/FCM infrastructure is system-level allowlisted in Android. Google Play Services maintains a persistent connection to Google's servers on behalf of all apps. When a high-priority FCM message arrives, the system: (1) temporarily lifts doze restrictions for the target app, (2) grants a partial wakelock for approximately 10 seconds, (3) allows network access during that brief window, and (4) delivers the message to your FirebaseMessagingService.onMessageReceived(). After ~10 seconds, doze snaps back. This is why FCM high-priority is the only reliable way to wake an app during deep doze — and why you should not try to do heavy work in that 10-second window. Enqueue a WorkManager task for the actual processing.
hints: Think about what makes FCM different from a regular network request during doze
Google Play Services has special system-level privileges that third-party apps don't
